title: Greed, Gambling, and Godliness
speaker: Elder Nick Anderson
source_files: Greed Gambling and Godliness (Elder Nick Anderson).txt

outline.title: Introduction: Gambling's Rising Prevalence
subpoints: Super Bowl Sunday is the single largest gambling event in the world — approximately $1.8 billion bet on one game
55-60% of adults gambled in some form in 2024; 23% participated in sports betting; 28% of US adults gamble online every day
Gambling revenue exceeds $71 billion per year; gambling ads are now inescapable
The Bible does not say 'you shall not gamble' explicitly — but it says a great deal about money, the heart, and wisdom that illuminates the question

outline.title: 1 Timothy 6: Godliness with Contentment
subpoints: Verse 6: 'godliness with contentment is great gain' — gambling appeals to the opposite: the promise that winning is great gain
Verses 8-9: 'having food and clothing, with these we shall be content' — the text does not even mention shelter; those who desire to be rich fall into temptation and a snare
Verse 10: 'the love of money is a root of all kinds of evil; some have strayed from the faith in their greediness' — the desire to be rich may cost you your soul
Verses 11-12: 'flee these things and pursue righteousness, godliness, faith, love, patience, gentleness; fight the good fight of faith'

outline.title: Ten Reasons Gambling Is Unwise
subpoints: 1. The Bible never commands getting rich quick — Proverbs 12:11: 'he who tills his land will be satisfied with bread; he who follows frivolity is devoid of understanding'
2. The Bible commands diligence and work — Proverbs 6:6-11: the sluggard is sent to the ant; poverty comes on the lazy like an armed man
3. We are commanded to plan wisely — a wise man provides an inheritance; no competent wealth manager would recommend gambling as part of a financial plan
4. We are commanded to be good stewards — God has given us what we have and we are responsible for it, whether much or little
5. Gambling erodes self-control — Proverbs 25:28; an estimated 5 million compulsive gamblers in the US, fewer than 10% seeking help
6. Gambling is a zero-sum game — it requires everyone else to lose for you to win; the poorest are the most likely to gamble
7. Gambling fosters love of money — the primary reason anyone gambles is to win money; to pretend otherwise is self-deception
8. We are commanded to give generously — Ephesians 4:28: labor so that you have something to give to those in need
9. Gambling is based on greed and covetousness — Michael Jordan's words: 'I want your money in my pocket'; that is gambling in a sentence

10. Gambling denies that God is sovereign — the gambler trusts in chance, luck, and fate; statistical probabilities are part of God's creation; those who do not tithe share this same unbelief in God's provision

outline.title: What to Pursue Instead
subpoints: 1 Timothy 6:11: pursue righteousness, godliness, faith, love, patience, gentleness — the whole of life should be a pursuit of these things
Proverbs 11:4: 'riches do not profit in the day of wrath, but righteousness delivers from death' — if we are pursuing riches, there is reason to repent today
Christ redeemed us to make us more like him, not more comfortable; the pursuit of wealth is one of the greatest distractions from that

1 Timothy 6:17-19: command those who are rich (everyone in America, historically) not to be haughty nor to trust in uncertain riches but in the living God; be rich in good works, ready to give, willing to share

outline.title: Closing Questions for Reflection
subpoints: Where is your faith? Who and what are you trusting when you buy a lottery ticket or place a bet?
Are you allowing covetousness and greed to rule your heart?
Is gambling a good use of the resources God has entrusted to you?
Are there broader ways you are failing to be a good steward of money, time, and talents?

summary:
Preached on Super Bowl Sunday 2025 — the single largest gambling event in the world — Elder Nick Anderson addresses whether Christians should gamble. He opens with striking statistics: over half of US adults gambled in 2024, 28% gamble online every day, and gambling revenue exceeds $71 billion annually. While the Bible never says 'you shall not gamble,' it says a great deal about the heart's attitude toward money, and 1 Timothy 6:6-19 forms the backbone of the sermon.

The elder presents ten reasons gambling is unwise: it contradicts the Bible's commands to work diligently, plan wisely, maintain self-control, love our neighbors, and trust God's sovereign ordering of the world. The most serious concern is reason ten — gambling denies that God is actually in control. Statistical probabilities are part of God's creation; the gambler trusts in chance and fate rather than in divine providence. He draws a provocative parallel to non-tithing, which reflects the same distrust in God's promise to bless those who honor him.

The sermon ends with a call not merely to abstain from gambling but to actively pursue righteousness, godliness, faith, love, patience, and gentleness (1 Timothy 6:11), and to be rich in good works rather than in uncertain riches. The congregation is reminded that by any global or historical standard, everyone in the room is rich, and therefore all of 1 Timothy 6:17-19 applies directly to them.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: 1 Timothy 6:6-19

*Now godliness with contentment is great gain. For we brought nothing into this world, and it is certain we can carry nothing out. And having food and clothing, with these we shall be content. But those who desire to be rich fall into temptation and a snare, and into many foolish and harmful lusts which drown men in destruction and perdition. For the love of money is a root of all kinds of evil, for which some have strayed from the faith in their greediness, and pierced themselves through with many sorrows.*

*But you, O man of God, flee these things and pursue righteousness, godliness, faith, love, patience, gentleness. Fight the good fight of faith, lay hold on eternal life, to which you were also called and have confessed the good confession in the presence of many witnesses. I urge you in the sight of God who gives life to all things, and before Christ Jesus who witnessed the good confession before Pontius Pilate, that you keep this commandment without spot, blameless until our Lord Jesus Christ's appearing...*

*Command those who are rich in this present age not to be haughty, nor to trust in uncertain riches but in the living God, who gives us richly all things to enjoy. Let them do good, that they may be rich in good works, ready to give, willing to share, storing up for themselves a good foundation for the time to come, that they may lay hold on eternal life.*

## Introduction: Super Bowl Sunday

So I hinted at it earlier today, but today is Super Bowl Sunday. I'm really going to take my time this afternoon — we are going to go through this passage word by word in the original Greek. You are going to be Greek masters by the end of it. Or annoyed with me. We'll see.

There actually is some significance to today being Super Bowl Sunday, because the Super Bowl happens to be the single largest gambling event in the world. And that is the topic of this afternoon's sermon: greed, gambling, and godliness.

I did not plan to preach on gambling on Super Bowl Sunday because I knew the date. I actually found that out during my sermon prep. So in God's providence, I am preaching on gambling and the love of money on the single largest gambling day in the world.

If you watch sports, or listen to sports talk radio, or even just exist, you have probably noticed that gambling has become more and more prevalent in our culture over the last five years. Growing up, the only gambling I was aware of was the Washington State Lottery. Sports betting was not allowed. There were no casinos to speak of. Today, it is virtually impossible to get away from ads for the lottery, for sports betting, for casinos. It is on every city bus.

Here are some statistics. Approximately 55-60% of adults participated in some form of gambling in 2024. That is over half of us — statistically speaking, not accusing anyone specifically. 23% of adults participated in sports betting in 2024. And this one really caught me: 28% of US adults gamble every day online. Almost a third of the population gambles daily. Gambling revenue is over $71 billion per year, and an estimated $1.8 billion will be bet on the Super Bowl today. That is just legal betting.

Because gambling has become so prevalent, we need to ask: what does the Bible say about gambling? Should Christians participate?

The Bible does not explicitly say "you shall not gamble." But I think the Bible has a great deal to say about money, about the heart's attitude toward money, and it gives us many reasons why we should not be involved in gambling.

## 1 Timothy 6: Godliness with Contentment

The passage we read begins: *Now godliness with contentment is great gain.* The person who gambles says to themselves: winning the lottery is great gain. That is exactly how gambling is advertised. The Washington State Lottery calls itself the "Department of Imagination" and invites you to imagine all the amazing things you could do if you won the jackpot — a fancy car, a bowling alley in your house, travel the world.

Gambling appeals to the side of us that is greedy and covetous. It tells us that what we have — what God has given us — is not good enough. It fosters discontentment.

The Bible says in verses 7-8: *having food and clothing, with these we shall be content.* Think about what is not mentioned there. Shelter. With food and clothing we shall be content. And then verse 9: *those who desire to be rich fall into temptation and a snare and into many foolish and harmful lusts which drown men in destruction and perdition.* If you get nothing else from today's sermon, get this: the desire to be rich may cost you your soul. The Scriptures tell us that some have strayed from the faith in their greediness. If you are gambling because you want to be rich, you need to repent.

## Ten Reasons Gambling Is Unwise

**Reason 1: The Bible never commands getting rich quick.**

Proverbs 12:11: *He who tills his land will be satisfied with bread, but he who follows frivolity is devoid of understanding.* The proverb teaches that diligent work produces results. Playing the lottery is exactly the opposite — the lure of gambling is largely the promise of eliminating the need to work. The Bible does not hold that up as a virtue.

**Reason 2: The Bible commands diligence and work.**

Proverbs 6:6-11: *Go to the ant, you sluggard! Consider her ways and be wise... how long will you slumber, O sluggard? A little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to sleep — so shall your poverty come on you like a prowler.* The gambler does not want to work diligently. Many dream of getting rich so they can quit their job. But Adam was commanded to work before the fall. Work is not the result of sin — it is part of how God made us. Diligence over time, as a general rule, produces results.

**Reason 3: We are commanded to plan wisely.**

Proverbs tells us a wise man provides an inheritance for his children. How does gambling fit into that? If you go to a wealth manager and a major part of their strategy is to gamble on the lottery, find a different wealth manager.

**Reason 4: We are commanded to be good stewards.**

Imagine if the session and deacons stood up and announced we had spent all the building fund on lottery tickets, hoping to hit the jackpot. You would rightly throw us out. Whether God has given you much or little, you are responsible for what you have been given. A famous YouTuber bought $1 million in lottery tickets and lost $300,000 net. You might say: well, spending a lot on gambling is foolish, but spending a little isn't a big deal. But the amount is irrelevant to the responsibility. We are stewards of whatever God has entrusted to us.

**Reason 5: Gambling erodes self-control.**

Proverbs 25:28: *Whoever has no rule over his own spirit is like a city broken down without walls.* Even gambling advertisements acknowledge this — they always include a hotline number. "Keep it fun." "If you have a problem, call this number." There are an estimated 5 million compulsive gamblers in the US, and fewer than 10% of them will ever seek help. Like any sin you toy with, it can become life-destroying.

**Reason 6: Gambling is a zero-sum game.**

Gambling requires everyone else to lose for you to win. At a minimum, that is a failure to love your neighbor. And gambling is far more prevalent among the poor than the rich — the people who can least afford to lose are the most likely to gamble.

**Reason 7: Gambling fosters love of money.**

The primary reason people gamble is to win money. Let's not fool ourselves. If you wager on a game today and you hope to lose, you need your head examined. The lure of gambling is quick riches. And we have seen in the text that the love of money is a root of all kinds of evil.

**Reason 8: We are commanded to give generously.**

Ephesians 4:28: *Let him who stole steal no longer, but rather let him labor, working with his hands what is good, that he may have something to give to him who has need.* Notice the purpose of work: not to hoard for retirement, but to have something to give away. That is what the Scriptures command.

**Reason 9: Gambling is based on greed and covetousness.**

There is a story about Michael Jordan on a team flight. He was gambling at the back of the plane — high-stakes poker, $10,000 a hand. The rookies were at the front playing for $5. Jordan came to the front and joined them. One of them asked why. He said: "I want your money in my pocket."

I cannot think of a better summary of gambling. I want your money in my pocket. That is it. And it is a failure to love your neighbor because all you are doing is hoping they lose so you can win.

**Reason 10: Gambling denies that God is sovereign.**

God has established the world and how it works. Statistical probabilities are part of creation. When a person buys a lottery ticket, they know the odds are extraordinarily against them — you are more likely to be struck by lightning on a clear day than to win the lottery. And yet they buy it anyway. Why? Because the gambler prefers to deny that God has established how the world works. The gambler does not trust in God. The gambler trusts in chance, luck, and fate.

A person who sits down at a poker table is trying to beat the odds that are stacked against them. They are trusting in chance rather than in the God who sovereignly governs all things. If we believe that God is in control and has established the laws by which the universe is governed, gambling should have no place in our lives.

As an aside, this is also why people fail to tithe. Proverbs 3:9-10: *Honor the Lord with your possessions, and with the firstfruits of your increase; so your barns will be filled with plenty, and your vats will overflow with new wine.* God has ordered the world so that those who honor him with their firstfruits will be blessed. To refuse is to deny that God's promises are true. The gambler and the non-tither are not so different — both show a lack of faith that what God has said is actually true.

## What to Do Instead

The Bible not only gives warnings about loving money — it tells us what to do instead. Verse 11: *But you, O man of God, flee these things and pursue righteousness, godliness, faith, love, patience, gentleness.* Our whole lives ought to be the pursuit of these things. Does gambling help in that pursuit? It does not.

Proverbs 11:4: *Riches do not profit in the day of wrath, but righteousness delivers from death.* If you are pursuing riches — whether by gambling or by any other means — there is reason to repent today. Christ has redeemed us to make us more like him, not more comfortable. The pursuit of wealth is one of the greatest distractions from that.

The passage ends: *Command those who are rich in this present age...* That is everyone here. People who live in the United States are richer than any other population in the entire history of the world. We are the rich. We are commanded not to be haughty, nor to trust in uncertain riches, but in the living God who gives us richly all things to enjoy. Let us do good, be rich in good works, ready to give, willing to share, storing up for ourselves a good foundation for the time to come.

By worldly standards, we may or may not think of ourselves as rich. But all of us — no matter what our income — can be rich in good works. That is what we are commanded to do. Focus your efforts on that. Trust in the Lord and not in uncertain riches.

*Dear Lord, we thank you for your goodness to us. We thank you for your Word. We pray, Lord, that you would work what is good in our hearts today. We ask this in Christ's name. Amen.*
